entry.title: Building AI Skills for the Future of the SAP Ecosystem
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence in the SAP ecosystem is entering a new chapter. We’ve moved from analytics to predictions, from predictions to generative AI, and now to Agentic AI; and this is where it gets truly exciting.

What Is Agentic AI?

Agentic AI goes beyond generating text or answering prompts. It can pursue goals, plan, decide, interact with systems, and execute multi-step actions within defined guardrails. While generative AI creates content, agentic AI drives outcomes, which is what matters most in enterprise environments.

In SAP, where processes connect finance, supply chain, procurement, and HR, this shift has enormous potential. SAP landscapes are structured, data-rich, and process-driven, ideal for intelligent agents that actively support and orchestrate business execution.

Imagine AI not only spotting a supply chain risk but triggering alternative sourcing workflows or detecting anomalies in financial postings and initiating clarification tasks automatically. This isn’t science fiction, it’s the direction enterprise AI is heading: from assistance to collaboration.

Under SAP’s Business AI strategy, AI is becoming embedded directly into applications and workflows. The vision is clear: AI as a digital co-worker. Not replacing people but augmenting them. Not adding complexity but reducing friction. Agentic AI is the next evolution in making that vision real.

 

 

Want to dive deeper into Agentic AI? Discover the mini agent blog series by @shannabauer and @christian_karaschewitz:

  • Envision the Future of Generative AI with SAP's AI Agents
  • AI Agents in Action
  • AI Agents - Thinking Fast & Thinking Slow, Balancing Autonomy and Determinism in Joule agents

 

Learning and Hands-On Opportunities for All Things AI

If you’re curious about how to identify real business impact, the course “Discovering High-Value Opportunities for Agentic AI” helps bridge innovation and value creation; a crucial skill as AI initiatives become more strategic. For a practical, hands-on introduction to working with intelligent agents, the Devtoberfest 2025 session “Getting Started with Agents using SAP Generative AI Hub” walks through the basics of building and experimenting with agent scenarios. Explore the full AI playlist for more resources with SAP Tech Bytes, Ask the Expert sessions and more on the SAP Developers YouTube channel. And for even more AI news, check out the monthly blog series that spotlights the latest AI learning assets and upcoming learning opportunities.

And the learning journey doesn’t stop there. SAP also offers several upcoming free live sessions open to anyone to bring AI innovation directly into practical enterprise scenarios:

  • Efficient Document Inbound Processing in SAP Cloud ERP with SAP Document AI – March 12, 2026: Discover how SAP Document AI streamlines inbound document handling in SAP Cloud ERP, reducing manual effort and increasing automation in finance and procurement processes.
  • All Things Business AI: Latest Insights, Updates & Live Demos – March 19, 2026: Get first-hand insights into SAP’s Business AI strategy, explore the newest updates, and experience live demonstrations of AI capabilities embedded across the SAP portfolio.
  • Build AI Securely – Avoid Pitfalls in the Development and Operations Lifecycle – April 9, 2026: Learn how to design, develop, and operate AI solutions securely, while avoiding common risks in governance, compliance, and lifecycle management.

 

Ready to dive deeper? Don’t miss the upcoming local SAP CodeJam events, where you can get hands-on experience with Generative AI Hub, SAP Joule, and AI-powered applications:

  • March 16, 2026:  Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Risch-Rotkreuz, Switzerland)
  • March 17, 2026:  Joule Studio: Create Joule Skills and Agents Without Coding (Risch-Rotkreuz, Switzerland)
  • March 20, 2026: Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Madrid, Spain)
  • March 20, 2026:  Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Belo Horizonte, Brazil)
  • April 7, 2026: ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (São Paulo, Brazil)
  • April 8, 2026:  ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (Londrina, Brazil)
  • April 10, 2026: Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Curitiba, Brazil)
  • April 10, 2026: SAP HANA Cloud for AI Applications: ML, Vectors, and Knowledge Graphs (Ribeirão Preto, Brazil)
  • And much more – check out the full list of all upcoming events here.

 

AI Literacy: Core Competency for SAP Professionals

Here’s the most important part: you don’t need to be a data scientist to participate in this transformation. What you do need is AI literacy. Understanding the fundamentals of artificial intelligence, generative AI, and how SAP AI services integrate into enterprise processes is quickly becoming a core competency for SAP professionals.

For developers ready to build AI-powered applications, the SAP Generative AI Developer certification validates hands-on expertise in designing and integrating generative AI within SAP environments. For consultants and presales professionals shaping customer conversations, the Positioning SAP Business AI Solutions as Part of the SAP Business Suite certification strengthens the ability to translate AI capabilities into business value.

Agentic AI represents a shift from “What can AI tell me?” to “What can AI achieve with me?”

For those working in the SAP ecosystem, this is more than a trend — it’s the next capability layer on top of digital transformation. The professionals who understand how to design, position, and work alongside AI agents will shape the next generation of enterprise processes.

The future of SAP is intelligent. The future of AI is agentic. And the opportunity to grow with it starts now.

entry.title: Service Order Determines Wrong Plant for Service Part (1010 instead of 1020) – SAP S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am currently testing a Service Management scenario in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and encountered an issue when releasing a Service Order containing a Service Part item.

Scenario

  • Service Order created in Company Code 1020

  • The Service Plant determined in the service order is 1020

  • The service order contains a Service Part item (Generator Oil)

However, when attempting to release the service order, the system throws the following error:

Intercompany scenario is not supported for service parts.
The plant for the current service part item is assigned to company code 1010.

The confusing part is that the service order itself correctly determines plant 1020, but during validation the system still determines plant 1010 for the service part, which causes a cross-company posting (1010 → 1020) and prevents the order from being released.

Questions

  1. From where does SAP determine the plant used for service parts in a service order?

  2. Could this be coming from:

    • Product master plant data

    • Employee service location

    • Service team

    • Default plant determination

  3. What configuration ensures that service parts inherit the same plant as the service order (1020)?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Transitioning from Chaos to Clarity: Converting Raw Design Worksheet XML to Excel
entry.content:
In the world of SAP SuccessFactors Compensation, XML is our foundation. Whether you are looking at the Compensation Template, or any raw XML, it can feel like a "jungle" of tags making it difficult to read, comprehend, or even analyze.

If you find yourself having 100+ fields/columns in a design worksheet and having to track and manage config points manually, it is time to build a better routine.

Currently, SAP does not have a tool within Comp Home to export Excel csv or xlxs for Config points in XML especially for Design Worksheet.

What if I told you there is a better way to manage this by converting Raw XML to Excel? When you do this, all the config points will be available in Excel with the field names, formulas, EC mappings, etc., for you to analyze and correct on the go.

Key Benefits:

  • Time Efficiency: You no longer have to manually update the Design Worksheet tab in the workbooks, which is time-consuming.
  • Data Accuracy: Ensures accurate tracking, as manual updates of workbooks especially for your client to track every cycle is prone to errors.
  • Troubleshooting: Finding and troubleshooting formula columns and find the root cause if you have many complex formulas. These by nature take time to analyse the more complex the formula.

Steps:

Once you extract the XML template from Advanced Settings, we need to prepare the file before we load it to Excel.

1. Prepare the Environment:
Create a new document in Notepad++ and change the Language to XML.

2. Setup the Wrapper:
Paste the following code in the blank XML (Snippet 1). In the code below, Line 2 is blank; this is where we will insert the design worksheet part of the XML from the extract.

Snippet 1

<comp-config>
 
</comp-config>

3. Identify the Design Worksheet Logic:
In your exported file, find the first instance of a group block which will start with '<comp-field-group id=' and select all the way until you find the '<comp-pay-matrix>' tag. This is where the design worksheet XML typically ends.

In this example, I have taken a small group block which has its label, translation, and 2 fields under it: Employee ID and Job Title. It also has many attributes like id, isCustomField, isVisible, etc. All these will generate in separate columns in Excel.

Snippet 2

<comp-field-group id="empInfoFields" useFor="salary" displayOrder="18" backgroundColor="AEAEAE" displayColumn="">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Employee Information]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-label lang="fr_FR"><![CDATA[Informations Employé]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-definition id="Employee_ID" isCustomField="true" isVisible="true" hideInProfile="false" hideInForm="false" type="string" useFor="salary" readOnly="false" width="6" hidePercentage="false" hideAmount="false" percentageReadOnly="false" reloadable="false" auditable="false" displayOrder="18">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Employee ID]]></comp-field-label>
</comp-field-definition>
<comp-field-definition id="jobTitle" isCustomField="false" isVisible="true" hideInProfile="false" hideInForm="false" alignment="center" useFor="salary" readOnly="false" width="6" hidePercentage="false" hideAmount="false" percentageReadOnly="false" reloadable="true" auditable="false" displayOrder="18">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Job Title]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-label lang="fr_FR"><![CDATA[Titre]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-ect-input-field-map componentType="jobInfo" fieldName="job-title"/>
</comp-field-definition>
</comp-field-group>

Important! Ensure all tags have supporting end tags or the Excel import won't work. Also, make sure all '<comp-field-definition>' tags are under '<comp-field-group>'. If fields are independent, you must add a group tag manually to club them, as shown below.

Snippet 3 (Incorrect/Independent Fields here starting from line 8 job title field is out of the group tag)

<comp-field-group id="empInfoFields" useFor="salary" displayOrder="18" backgroundColor="AEAEAE" displayColumn="">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Employee Information]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-label lang="fr_FR"><![CDATA[Informations Employé]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-definition id="Employee_ID" isCustomField="true" isVisible="true" hideInProfile="false" hideInForm="false" type="string" useFor="salary" readOnly="false" width="6" hidePercentage="false" hideAmount="false" percentageReadOnly="false" reloadable="false" auditable="false" displayOrder="18">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Employee ID]]></comp-field-label>
</comp-field-definition>
</comp-field-group>
<comp-field-definition id="jobTitle" isCustomField="false" isVisible="true" hideInProfile="false" hideInForm="false" alignment="center" useFor="salary" readOnly="false" width="6" hidePercentage="false" hideAmount="false" percentageReadOnly="false" reloadable="true" auditable="false" displayOrder="18">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Job Title]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-label lang="fr_FR"><![CDATA[Titre]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-ect-input-field-map componentType="jobInfo" fieldName="job-title"/>
</comp-field-definition>

Snippet 4 (Corrected by adding a group tag in line 9 and an ending group tag in line 15 manually)

<comp-field-group id="empInfoFields" useFor="salary" displayOrder="18" backgroundColor="AEAEAE" displayColumn="">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Employee Information]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-label lang="fr_FR"><![CDATA[Informations Employé]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-definition id="Employee_ID" isCustomField="true" isVisible="true" hideInProfile="false" hideInForm="false" type="string" useFor="salary" readOnly="false" width="6" hidePercentage="false" hideAmount="false" percentageReadOnly="false" reloadable="false" auditable="false" displayOrder="18">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Employee ID]]></comp-field-label>
</comp-field-definition>
</comp-field-group>
 
<comp-field-group>
<comp-field-definition id="jobTitle" isCustomField="false" isVisible="true" hideInProfile="false" hideInForm="false" alignment="center" useFor="salary" readOnly="false" width="6" hidePercentage="false" hideAmount="false" percentageReadOnly="false" reloadable="true" auditable="false" displayOrder="18">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Job Title]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-label lang="fr_FR"><![CDATA[Titre]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-ect-input-field-map componentType="jobInfo" fieldName="job-title"/>
</comp-field-definition>
</comp-field-group>

Here, I have shown example of only one field. If you have multiple ungrouped fields, identify all of them and put the field blocks one below the other between this manually created group tag block.

We do this to ensure all fields align within the same columns in Excel. Without a group tag, Excel treats independent fields as separate data sets, creating additional columns that are redundant causing inconsistent reporting. Grouping them manually ensures unified layout and a cleaner reporting.

4. Finalize and Load:
Paste the final selected code into the Notepad++ document where we copied Snippet 1 having <comp-config> tags and add the code in between the <comp-config> tags as below and save it, and open Excel.

<comp-config>
<comp-field-group id="empInfoFields" useFor="salary" displayOrder="18" backgroundColor="AEAEAE" displayColumn="">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Employee Information]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-label lang="fr_FR"><![CDATA[Informations Employé]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-definition id="Employee_ID" isCustomField="true" isVisible="true" hideInProfile="false" hideInForm="false" type="string" useFor="salary" readOnly="false" width="6" hidePercentage="false" hideAmount="false" percentageReadOnly="false" reloadable="false" auditable="false" displayOrder="18">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Employee ID]]></comp-field-label>
</comp-field-definition>
</comp-field-group>

<comp-field-group>
<comp-field-definition id="jobTitle" isCustomField="false" isVisible="true" hideInProfile="false" hideInForm="false" alignment="center" useFor="salary" readOnly="false" width="6" hidePercentage="false" hideAmount="false" percentageReadOnly="false" reloadable="true" auditable="false" displayOrder="18">
<comp-field-label><![CDATA[Job Title]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-field-label lang="fr_FR"><![CDATA[Titre]]></comp-field-label>
<comp-ect-input-field-map componentType="jobInfo" fieldName="job-title"/>
</comp-field-definition>
</comp-field-group>
</comp-config>

In Excel, enable the Developer Tab (File > Options > Customize Ribbon). Go to the Developer Tab once enabled. You will now find the XML section. Click Import.

The XML is now loaded into Excel columns for easy tracking!

5. Refreshing Data:
If there are any changes to the source XML, simply use the Refresh Data button in the XML section.

Limitations & Troubleshooting:

You can only upload unique XML files in one sheet. If you try to import the same XML structure into Sheet 2, it won't load. In this case:

  • Go to the Source option in the XML Section.
  • Go to XML Maps and click Add.

Finally, click on comp-config in the XML source, drag and drop it onto the sheet, and click Refresh Data.

Conclusion:

Overall, there are many methods to convert XML to Excel which can be explored. So stop manually tracking your design worksheet. This method is not just restricted to SF Comp but anything that uses XML. The lines need to be identified, prepared and extracted based on your needs to organize XML to meaningful coherent data. Importing the whole XML in Excel may sometimes be inconsistent and tackling them part by part is recommended for Reporting. Until we have the option to convert config points in XML to csv or xlxs, we can use this technique.

entry.title: How to Connect On-Premise ABAP System to SAP BTP (Business Application Studio)-BAS
entry.content:
SAP BTP (Business Application Studio):

In modern SAP architectures, customers build Fiori, RAP, and CAP applications on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) while keeping their ERP systems on-premise. To enable secure communication between SAP BTP and an on-premise ABAP system.

Prerequisites:

 1.SAP BTP Subaccount (Trial or Enterprise).

2.SAP Cloud Connector Installed.

3.On-Premises ABAP System Details.

Important:
Cloud Connector establishes an outbound encrypted connection. No inbound firewall ports are required.
Step-by-Step Configuration: Create Destination in BTP.
Step 1: Open SAP BTP Cockpit:  Open your browser and navigate to:  https://cockpit.hanatrial.ondemand.com/trial/#/home/trial   

This blog does not cover how to create a SAP BTP account, as many videos and tutorials and official SAP documentation already explain that process in detail.

Before proceeding, ensure:

  • You have access to an SAP BTP Global Account
  • A Subaccount is already created
  • Connectivity and Destination services are enabled
  • SAP Business Application Studio is subscribed

Once these prerequisites are completed, continue with the Cloud Connector configuration.

 Click on Go To Your Trail Account:

 After That Click On   Trail Button.

Click On Destination Button:

Click on Create Button:

 Select From Scratch Option and Provide  to   The Your On-Premises ABAP System   Detail:

Note: Username and Password  should be your On-Premise ABAP System

Example: 

Note:  While creating the Destination in SAP BTP, make sure the URL field is correctly.

  • Your Virtual Host name
  • The Port number
  • The correct protocol prefix
    Example: http://nayakhana.com:8899

The http:// prefix is mandatory.

After That Please click on Add Property  and Give to the Below Details .

Note:    Hear sap-client---> 100 means this  is  your On-Premise ABAP System Client Number.

Example:

Once you have maintained all required details of your On-Premise ABAP system in the Destination configuration:

  • URL (Virtual Host + Port)
  • Proxy Type = OnPremise
  • Authentication = BasicAuthentication
  • Backend Username
  • Backend Password
  • Additional Properties (sap-client, etc.)
  • Click on “Check Connection”.

If you have maintained all configuration details correctly in  SAP BTP Destination, and you click Check Connection, you may see the message:"Backend is reachable from the Cloud Connector".

This confirms that the first step of BTP Destination connectivity is completed successfully.

Please Go  to The  Below link For:

 Step 2-  SAP Cloud Connector.
https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-connect-sap-cloud-connector-to-sap-btp/ba-p/143… 
Step 3:   Connect BAS To  SAP On-Premise System:

https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/connect-sap-business-application-studio-bas-to-on-premise-abap-system/ba-p/14340482 



Thank You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak.

entry.title: How Do I Activate a Situation Type in the Extended Framework?
entry.content:
Situation Handling notifies users about issues requiring their attention. You may choose SAP delivered situation templates or design your own use cases. For the extended framework for Situation Handling, we often discussed how you can create custom situation templates. The framework comes also with SAP delivered use cases that you can easily activate.

This blog post explains how you can activate situation types from SAP delivered situation templates with the extended framework for Situation Handling. It has been available since SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2202 and SAP S/4HANA 2021 FPS02.

 

SAP Delivered Use Cases

Looking for Situation Handling use cases? Visit SAP Help and navigate to Situation Handling, then choose Use Cases. You can narrow your results by filtering for Situation Template Type (either extended object-based or message-based), area, and topic. To learn more about a specific use case, simply check the link in the Situation Template column for a detailed description.

Use Case

For illustration purposes we use the situation template Expiration of Purchase Contracts (PROC_ABOUT_TO_EXPIRE_CTR). Purchasers get a heads-up for purchase contracts that are about to expire.

 

Activation Overview for 2SL and 3SL

Before discussing the activation steps in detail, let me point out the differences between system landscapes and their specific transport paths.

In the extended framework for Situation Handling, you create a situation type from a situation template. Situation templates are only visible in the quality system of a 2-system landscape (2SL) and the development system of a  3-system-landscape (3SL). The situation type needs to be transported to the production system. The transport paths differ between 2SL and 3SL.

Transport the Situation Type in 2SL

In a 2-system-landscape, you transport the situation type from the quality system to the production system.

  • In the quality system you
    • Create a situation type from a situation template
    • Test if the configuration alerts the right users about the situation
    • Export the well-defined situation type
  • In the production system you
    • Import the situation type
    • Since the data usually differs between quality and production system you need to enter the appropriate values for the condition filters
    • Enable the situation type

Transports in 2-System-Landscape

Transport the Situation Type in 3SL

In a 3-system-landscape, transports are required from the development system to both the test and production system. In a 3SL environment you may also have different tenants for development, customizing, and parallel customizing that need to be considered as well.

  • On the customizing tenant in the development system you
    • Create a situation type from a situation template
    • Export the situation type
  • On the test tenant in the test system you
    • Import the situation type
    • Since the data usually differs between systems you need to enter the appropriate values for the condition filters
    • Test if the configuration alerts the right users about the situation
    • Forward the well-defined situation type
  • On the production tenant in the production system you
    • Import the forwarded situation type
    • Enter the appropriate values for the condition filters
    • Enable the situation type

Transports in 3-System-Landscape

Detailed Activation of a Situation Type in 3SL

Now let’s check the detailed steps for a sample use case in a 3-system-landscape as it is more comprehensive. In 2SL the export / import mechanism resembles the one from development system to test system in 3SL.

Depending on your system setting, the transports may also be processed automatically. For optimal clarity, the blog post discusses the transport steps through manual triggers.

Development System

Create a Situation Type from a Situation Template

Log-in as business process configuration expert on the customizing tenant in the development system. Open the Manage Situation Types – Extended app where you see a list of SAP delivered templates. Use the search option to find the template for our use case Expiration of Purchase Contracts (PROC_ABOUT_TO_EXPIRE_CTR). Select the template and an object page opens. In the header, select Create Situation Type.

Create situation type from situation template

A new page opens with the situation type that contains all information from the situation template. You just need to enter an ID, Name and Description. The type is configured and ready to use. Depending on your requirements you could adapt the settings, for example, by adding filter values for the conditions, changing texts or rescheduling the batch job.

I recommend monitoring the instances to see how situations are handled.

Monitor situations

Let’s check the relevant settings and understand how the configuration works.

There are two Situation Triggers that detect situations and cover different usage scenarios. While the batch-based trigger checks for situations in the background, the event-based trigger reacts on every active change of the contract.

Situation triggers

The situation triggers cover the following cases:

  • The Event-Based Trigger checks the conditions whenever you create or update a purchase contract.
    • Condition 1 checks if the validity period of a purchase contract expires within the next 30 days and triggers a situation. The information displayed to the user is defined in Situation Display number 1.
    • Condition 2 is only executed if condition 1 did not match. That is, the validity period is not within the next 30 days, for instance when the contract got extended, and the situation is closed. As nobody will be informed about the closure, the texts are empty (Situation Display number 2).

Event-based trigger

  • In addition to the events, the Batch-Based Trigger regularly checks the validity end date according to the batch schedule.
    • Condition 1 checks if the validity period of a purchase contract expires within the next 30 days and triggers a situation.

Batch-based trigger

The configuration for the Batch Job Scheduling specifies that the contract dates are verified every 3 hours throughout the day, beginning at 6:00 a.m.

Batch job scheduling

Export the Situation Type

Log-in as administrator on the customizing tenant in the development system. Open the Export Software Collection app.

  • On top of the table choose Create Software Collection and a new page opens
  • Choose Add Items and select the situation type you created
  • On the header select Check and wait until the check is processed successfully
  • Then choose Export and wait until the export is finished successfully

Test System

Import the Situation Type

Log-in as administrator on the test tenant in the test system. Open the Import Collection app.

  • Choose your software collection and a new page opens
  • On the header select Manual Import and wait until the check is processed successfully
  • Then choose Import on the bottom and wait until the import is finished successfully

Test the Situation Type

As business process specialist you can test the situation type on the corresponding tenant.

Make sure that the situation type is enabled.

Activate the situation type

Then maintain the conditions. The filter values are not transported since they may differ in the various systems and tenants.

  • Select the Event-Based Trigger and a new page opens
    • Condition 1 select Edit and enter the filter values
      • Ext. Reference ID: enter Empty, using the value help
      • Validity Period End: enter Next30Days, using the value help
    • Condition 2 select Edit and enter the filter values
      • Ext. Reference ID: enter Empty, using the value help
    • Go back to the main page

Event-based trigger

  • Select the Batch-Based Trigger and a new page opens
    • Condition 1 select Edit and enter the filter values
      • Ext. Reference ID: enter Empty, using the value help
      • Validity Period End: enter Next30Days, using the value help
    • Go back to the main page

Batch-based trigger

With the situation triggers, you defined when a situation is detected. Now, you need to define who should be informed about situations. Maintain the team(s) in the Manage Teams and Responsibilities app and ensure that at least one team is enabled. The filter settings work the same way as with the standard framework for Situation Handling.

Situation recipients

Now you can test the situation type in your business area with the corresponding application data. You can check if there are purchase contracts that match the conditions by choosing Trigger Batch and Manage Instances.

Test the type with trigger batch

If no situations were detected you can log-in as purchaser and create or manipulate a purchasing contract. Add an end date within the next 30 days and the save event will trigger a situation.

When you log-in as a team member of the recipients team you can also check, if you were alerted about the situation.

After the successful test you push the situation type to the production system.

Forward the Situation Type

Log-in as administrator and open the Import Collection app.

  • Select your software collection and a new page opens
  • On the header select Forward and wait until the validation is processed successfully
  • Then choose Forward on the bottom and wait until the export is finished successfully

Production System

Import Collection to Production System

As administrator open the Import Collection app on the production tenant in the production system.

  • Choose your software collection and a new page opens
  • On the header select Manual Import and wait until the check is processed successfully
  • Then choose Import on the bottom and wait until the import is finished successfully

Activate the Situation Type

Log-in as business process specialist and open the Manage Situation Types – Extended app. Like in the test system, you need to maintain the situation type.

  • Enable the situation type
  • Add the filter values for the trigger conditions
  • Ensure that at least one recipient team is enabled

Now you are all set.

Further Information

You find many more blog posts in the Best Practices section of Intelligent Situation Handling in the SAP Community. And you can visit SAP Help Portal for further guidance on Situation Handling.

entry.title: Practical Lessons in the Development of Advanced RAG Architectures in SAP Business AI
entry.content:
The Challenge

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) has quickly become the backbone of modern enterprise AI. In the SAP ecosystem—where business processes rely on massive, complex, and varied documents—RAG isn't just a buzzword. It's a critical layer of contextual intelligence that makes corporate data actually usable.

When you get into real-world projects, you realize RAG is about much more than just building conversational chatbots. We’re talking about deep support assistants that can handle complex business workflows using unstructured data. Think along the lines of:

  • Assistants tackling highly technical documentation queries

  • Automated document validation in Procurement

  • Verification of mandatory contractual clauses

  • Intelligent support for financial and logistics operations

  • Contextual analysis of internal procedures and policies

In these scenarios, a "plausible" answer isn't good enough. The business needs precise, traceable, and reliable responses that meet strict enterprise standards. Moving from a flashy demo to a scalable, production-ready solution doesn't just depend on picking a good generative model. It comes down to two structural factors:

  • Ingestion quality (grounding): How we transform raw content into meaningful units that can be retrieved accurately.

  • Relevance of retrieved content (retrieval): The quality of the context we feed the LLM so it can generate the right answer.

This is exactly why a proof-of-concept might look great on day one, but fall apart in production. If your ingestion breaks document structure, or your retrieval misses the key details, the LLM is flying blind—leading to incomplete answers, confusion, and hallucinations. The real challenge isn't just technical; it's architectural and strategic.

 

There Is No Single “Winning” Architecture: The Toolbox Matters

A common trap when approaching RAG is searching for the one "perfect" combination of chunking, embeddings, vector stores, and LLMs. The reality? The right stack depends entirely on your use case.

Because of this, our team at AYESA didn't just try to guess the final architecture. Instead, we treated this as a rigorous process: comparing techniques side-by-side and measuring their real-world impact. The real value of this three-month exercise was figuring out:

  • Which technique actually adds value in a specific context

  • The hidden costs associated with each setup

  • The risks introduced by different methods (like loss of structure, added noise, or semantic degradation)


Starting Point: Quantitative Evaluation Framework

To cut through the noise and avoid subjective opinions ("this answer looks okay"), we set up a strict evaluation framework using RAGAS (https://docs.ragas.io). We built a control dataset of 11 highly complex technical questions alongside their ideal answers (Ground Truth).

We weighted our metrics to explicitly balance retrieval and generation, giving us a reliable final score:

  • Context Recall (40%): Does the retrieved context actually contain the necessary information? (This is critical).
  • Faithfulness (20%): Is the answer based strictly on the context, or is the model hallucinating
  • Answer Relevancy (20%): Does the answer actually address the user's intent?
  • Context Precision (20%): Does the most relevant information show up at the top of the search results?

To keep everything secure and governed, all generative interactions with the LLM were orchestrated through SAP AI Launchpad.

Evolution Sequence: From V1 to V7

During our research, we documented seven iterative versions. We defined each version by pairing a specific Grounding strategy (G) with a Retrieval strategy (R). You can see how our global score evolved here:

 

Here is a breakdown of our journey: the techniques, the tools, and why we got the results we did. Our base architecture relied on SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, and HANA Cloud.

 

V1 - Simple RAG (Baseline)

Technique:

  • Grounding (G1): fixed-size document chunking
  • Retrieval (R1): similarity search in SAP HANA Cloud, retrieving the top 3 chunks

Baseline score: 0.73

Value and Applicability: V1 is basically our minimum viable architecture. It’s great for getting a quick baseline and validating the end-to-end pipeline (ingestion, embeddings, vector store, retrieval, and generation).

Why It Falls Short: When dealing with complex documentation, fixed chunking is brutal—it breaks tables in half and separates closely tied concepts. Also, retrieving only 3 fragments limits your context recall, especially for questions that require the LLM to synthesize interconnected information.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V2 - Parent/Child Strategy

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Small “child” chunks for precise searching, mapped to larger “parent” chunks that preserve the full context.

  • Retrieval (R2): We search against the children, but pass the parents to the LLM for the final context.

Score: 0.78 👍

Value: This is a lifesaver when documents have strict hierarchies (sections, subsections, annexes). It boosts search precision without destroying the semantic flow.

Why It Improves: By retrieving the “parent,” the LLM isn't forced to guess based on isolated, out-of-context fragments. V2 gave us a much better balance between precision and having enough context to work with.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V3 - LLM-Generated Chunks and Keywords

Technique

  • Grounding (G3): LLM-generated chunks and keywords
  • Retrieval (R3): similarity search + keyword-based reordering

Score: 0.60 👎👎 (worse than V2)

Why It Failed Here:
Using an LLM for chunking introduced unexpected risks:

  • The model created segments that didn't match the actual semantic boundaries of the text.

  • The generated keywords often failed to accurately represent the highly technical domain. In this case, trying to add more "intelligence" actually degraded the quality.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V4 - Multi-Query + Reranker #1 (MS MARCO)

Technique

  • Grounding (G2):Back to the Parent/Child strategy.
  • Retrieval (R4): Generate 3 query variants and rerank the resulting chunks using MS MARCO.

Score: 0.66 👍

Why Improvement Was Limited: MS MARCO is a great general-purpose reranker, but that’s exactly the problem. In domains heavily loaded with SAP-specific terminology, it tends to reorder chunks based on generic semantics that misalign with the technical intent.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V5 - Reranker #2 (BGE)

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Parent/Child
  • Retrieval (R5): A broad search (Top 10) followed by specialized reranking using BGE, keeping the Top 3.

Score: 0.83 👍👍 (significant jump)

Why It Works: Casting a wider net during the initial retrieval dramatically improves Context Recall, while the specialized reranking fixes Context Precision. This one-two punch is highly effective for production-grade enterprise assistants.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V6 - Architecture with Cohere

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Parent/Child
  • Retrieval (R6): broad search (Top 10) + Cohere commercial reranker, keeping top 3.

Score: 0.84 👍 and Context Recall 95% 👍

Value: This setup maximizes coverage without overwhelming the LLM with useless context. Commercial rerankers like Cohere add a layer of robustness and generalization that is hard to beat.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V7 -  Final Architecture (SAP HANA Native Cross-Encoder)

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Parent/Child
  • Retrieval (R7): We swapped the third-party reranker for SAP’s native Cross-Encoder (December 2025).

Score: 0.87 👍  and Context Recall 100% 👍 👍 👍

Analysis: This was the game-changer. By using a reranker specifically trained and aligned with SAP’s enterprise domain, we maxed out our precision. Hitting 100% recall proved we had finally eliminated our retrieval blind spots.

 

Conclusions, Lessons Learned, and Best Practices

Ingestion Is Not a Detail: It Is Central Design

  • V1 → V2 confirms naive chunking is insufficient. Parent/Child acts as a quality safeguard.
  • Best practice: Preserve your hierarchies, tables, and dependencies. Do not just chunk text—chunk meaning.

Generalist Components May Fail in Technical Domains

  • V4 was a harsh reminder that generic rerankers won't necessarily beat strategies designed to respect domain specifics.
  • Best practice: Prioritize models aligned with your technical terminology, and measure everything with frameworks like RAGAS.

Winning Pattern: Broad Retrieval + Specialized Reranking

  • The progression from V5 to V7 proved this is the most reliable enterprise strategy.
  • Best practice: Investing time and resources into a good reranker is almost always more profitable than endlessly "prompt tuning" your LLM.

Explicit Metrics Prevent Subjective Debates

  • RAGAS gave us the objective truth. Weighting recall so heavily reflects a practical reality: if the model doesn't have the right context, it cannot give a safe answer.
  • Best practice: Define what "quality" means for your project upfront (faithfulness, recall, precision, relevance) and stick to it.

Alignment with the SAP Ecosystem

  • V7 proved that leaning into native SAP components not only simplifies the architecture but delivers superior performance.

Final Conclusion

This three-month deep dive by the SAP Business AI team at AYESA proves that excellence in enterprise RAG doesn't come from finding a "magic model." It comes from iterative architecture and ruthless, rigorous evaluation. Our evolution to V7 provides a solid blueprint for architects looking to deploy reliable, high-performance assistants on SAP #BTP.

 

@ChristophMorgen @mfath @mkemeter @thiago

entry.title: BM25 and Hybrid Search for RAG on SAP HANA Cloud (without PAL)
entry.content:
Introduction

Vector search is a common approach for RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines, but if you have worked with it you'll see that it's power is also a weakness sometimes... Normally you will make use of this type or search to find content by meaning but, what if you want to search for an exact mach? 

When a user asks about "article 12" or a document code like "HR-NORM-2024/0003", semantic search returns vaguely related content instead of the exact document. You need keyword search too. SAP HANA Cloud doesn't have built-in BM25 ranking without PAL (Predictive Analysis Library), which isn't always available or licensed. This post introduces langchain-hana-retriever, an open-source package I built that brings BM25 keyword search and hybrid retrieval to HANA Cloud without requiring PAL.

What it does

langchain-hana-retriever provides two LangChain-compatible retrievers:

  • HANABm25Retriever -- keyword search with BM25 scoring
  • HANAHybridRetriever -- combines vector search with BM25, merged via Reciprocal Rank Fusion

Both implement LangChain's BaseRetriever interface. They drop into any existing LangChain or LangGraph pipeline.

 

How BM25 works on HANA without PAL

LIKE doesn't work on NCLOB columns in HANA. Since most RAG setups store chunked text in NCLOB, standard substring matching fails. The workaround is LOCATE with TO_NVARCHAR:

 

SELECT "VEC_TEXT", "SOURCE"
FROM "MY_DOCS"
WHERE LOCATE(TO_NVARCHAR("VEC_TEXT"), 'article') > 0
   OR LOCATE(TO_NVARCHAR("VEC_TEXT"), '12') > 0
LIMIT 50

 

This fetches candidates, rows containing any of the query tokens. Then the rank-bm25 Python library scores them with BM25Okapi (term frequency, inverse document frequency, document length normalization). The result is a properly ranked list, not just "contains the string."

Installation

 

pip install langchain-hana-retriever

 

For hybrid retrieval (adds langchain-community for HanaDB vector store):

 

pip install "langchain-hana-retriever[hybrid]"

 

BM25 keyword retriever

 

import hdbcli.dbapi
from langchain_hana_retriever import HANABm25Retriever

connection = hdbcli.dbapi.connect(
    address="your-host.hanacloud.ondemand.com",
    port=443,
    user="DBADMIN",
    password="your-password",
    encrypt=True,
)

retriever = HANABm25Retriever(
    connection=connection,
    table_name="YOUR_TABLE",
    content_column="VEC_TEXT",
    metadata_columns=["SOURCE", "PAGE_START"],
    k=10,
    candidate_limit=50,
)

docs = retriever.invoke("article 12 reporting requirements")
for doc in docs:
    print(doc.metadata["SOURCE"], "-", doc.page_content[:100])

 

candidate_limit controls how many rows HANA returns before BM25 re-ranks. Set it higher than k (default 50 vs 10). max_tokens_in_query limits query tokens in the SQL WHERE clause (default 5) to avoid sending too many OR conditions.

Hybrid retriever

Vector search finds semantically similar content. BM25 finds exact matches. Hybrid gives you both.

 

from langchain_community.vectorstores import HanaDB
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_hana_retriever import HANABm25Retriever, HANAHybridRetriever

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

vector_store = HanaDB(
    connection=connection,
    embedding=embeddings,
    table_name="YOUR_TABLE",
)

keyword_retriever = HANABm25Retriever(
    connection=connection,
    table_name="YOUR_TABLE",
    k=10,
)

hybrid = HANAHybridRetriever(
    vector_store=vector_store,
    keyword_retriever=keyword_retriever,
    alpha=0.5,
    k=10,
)

docs = hybrid.invoke("eligibility requirements article 12")

 

alpha controls the balance: 1.0 = vector only, 0.0 = keyword only, 0.5 = equal weight. Results are merged with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF, k=60).

When to use what

  • Vector only: conceptual queries ("what are the eligibility requirements for subsidies")
  • BM25 only: exact lookups ("article 12", "HR-LIQUI-2024/0003", specific amounts)
  • Hybrid: queries mixing concepts with specific references ("eligibility requirements under article 12")

Hybrid with alpha=0.5 is a safe default for most RAG use cases.

Technical notes

  • LOCATE(TO_NVARCHAR(column), token) is the HANA workaround for NCLOB substring matching. TO_NVARCHAR has a default length limit -- candidate retrieval checks a truncated version, BM25 scoring uses the full text.
  • BM25 corpus is built per query from candidates. Works well for RAG (small candidate sets) but not designed for full-table ranking of millions of rows.
  • Async methods delegate to sync because hdbcli is a synchronous C-extension driver.
  • Deduplication in hybrid mode matches by page_content to avoid returning the same chunk from both sources.

Conclusion

If you're doing RAG on HANA Cloud and want better retrieval quality than vector search alone, this package gives you BM25 keyword ranking and hybrid search without needing PAL. Install it with pip install langchain-hana-retriever and try it on your existing HANA tables.

The package is MIT licensed and I welcome contributions. If you also need persistent agent state in HANA, check out the companion package langgraph-checkpoint-hana.

  • PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/langchain-hana-retriever/
  • GitHub: https://github.com/stubborncoder/langchain-hana-retriever

entry.title: Don’t Rush Configuration Without Knowing the Purpose
entry.content:
When I first started learning SAP FICO, I was very excited about configuration. I wanted to go into SPRO, create G/L accounts, define document types, and try everything quickly. It felt like I was making real progress just by completing configuration steps.

But as I continued learning, I realized something important: configuration only truly makes sense when we understand the business purpose behind it.

In SAP, settings are not just technical tasks. They represent how a real company runs its financial processes. For example, a G/L account is not just a number in the system, it shows how a company tracks its money. A posting key is not just a code ,it controls how transactions affect accounts. When I didn’t fully understand these purposes, I found myself memorizing steps instead of really learning.

I also noticed that rushing into configuration without understanding the business side can easily lead to mistakes. Wrong settings can cause incorrect postings, reporting issues, and confusion later when trying to fix problems.

Now, before I look at configuration, I try to ask myself:

  • What business process is this configuration supporting?
  • What is the financial impact of this setting?

When I focus on the purpose first, configuration becomes much more logical and easier to remember. It feels less like following steps and more like thinking the way an SAP consultant should think.

I’m still learning every day, but this mindset has helped me a lot. Take your time, understand the business, and then configure with confidence. @SAPSAP4  #FICO

entry.title: The 63% Barrier: Is Your Strategy Outpacing Your Talent?
entry.content:
The world is moving through a period of rapid technological acceleration that is doing more than just reshaping tools, but rather changing the DNA of jobs, industries, and entire economies. According to the World Economic Forum (2025)1 , 86% of employers expect AI and information processing technologies (including Big Data, VR, AR, etc.) to transform their business.

However, this transition has unveiled a critical threat to business success. While technology is the primary engine of transformation, the Future of Jobs Report reveals that 63% of employers view the skills gap as the single biggest barrier to progress. Today, only 2 out of 10 business leaders believe the current education system effectively develops the necessary AI and data skills.

In this high-stakes environment, equipping talent with the right skillset is no longer just an HR matter, but a strategic asset for global competitiveness and societal progress.

Bridging the “Readiness Gap”

While technology is the engine of transformation, 63% of employers view the skills gap as the biggest barrier to progress. To address this, SAP has invested in capacity-building programs like the SAP Young Professionals to support the SAP Ecosystem (including Partners and Customers) in securing the talent they need to differentiate themselves in the digital era.

Many platforms offer beginner-level AI certifications in as little as 30 hours 2. However, these often lack the industry alignment that matters most. Therefore, the SAP Young Professionals is designed as an intensive enablement journey, led by world-class experts and aligned strictly with the ecosystem’s needs to drastically reduce "ramp-up" time for new hires.

It is not enough to simply “know AI”. Success requires understanding the specific needs of Customers, who represent 99 of the 100 largest companies in the world. Consequently, the program focuses on the high-demand Core Skills for 2030 identified by the WEF:

  • Artificial Intelligence
  • Technological literacy (Encompassing digital skills and programming)
  • Soft skills such as: analytical thinking, leadership, resilience, agility, among other soft skills

 

A Story of Transformation

Once applicants complete the SAP Young Professionals, they get two Certifications as SAP Associate Consultants; validating that they are equipped to use, build and maintain the intelligent platforms of tomorrow, while also ensuring AI becomes a catalyst for efficiency.

I recently had the privilege of discussing the future of the ecosystem with a standout YPP alumni who embodies this shift and he is the answer to that 2/10 readiness barrier. In his own words, he shares how the YPP allowed him to merge technical S/4HANA expertise with the critical thinking required to navigate a digital transformation economy:

 

 

Cultivating the Next Era

The goal for the SAP ecosystem is clear: we must ensure our teams are prepared for rapid technological acceleration. By integrating SAP certified talent, organizations inject Cloud and AI-native stability into their digital roadmaps.

The future of Intelligent Enterprise is already here, and the secret to success is empowering the people ready to run it.

If you want to check out the pool of SAP Associate Consultants, please reach out to digitalskillscenter@sap.comç

____________________________________________________________________________________________________

I'd like to read you 👀 

What’s your strategy for overcoming the skills gap? Whether you are a partner, customer or SAP-related, let’s discuss how fresh, YPP-certified perspectives are changing the game in your region.

1. World Economic Forum. (2025). New Economy Skills: Building AI, Data and Digital Capabilities for Growth. *WEF_New_Economy_Skills_2025.pdf

2. World Economic Forum. (2025). Future of Jobs Report. WEF_Future_of_Jobs_Report_2025.pdf

entry.title: CX Onboarding: How to Prevent and Manage Project Risks Using SAP Best Practices
entry.content:
How to Prevent and Manage Project Risks Using SAP Best Practices

Implementing SAP Customer Experience (CX) solutions offers organizations a powerful opportunity to modernize processes, streamline operations, and strengthen customer engagement.

Read below to learn how customers and partners can avoid the most common project pitfalls, which we have grouped into three major risk areas: project‑related, people‑related, and security/compliance‑related risks.

Setting a Strong Foundation for Project Success and Managing Project‑Related Risks

Effective risk management starts long before configuration begins. One of the first steps is choosing the right implementation methodology to guide your project from initiation through day‑to‑day operations. The recommended approach is SAP Activate, which includes extensive training resources to help you become familiar with the framework. SAP also offers the SAP Activate Road Map Viewer (S‑User required), where you can access templates and assets that support every phase from Discover to Run.

Once you’ve selected the right methodology, your project is already on a strong footing. However, there are additional considerations to help you proactively prevent risks:

  • Perform upfront risk assessments and budget planning to avoid budget overruns.
  • Define a clear scope, timeline, and responsibility matrix
  • Document and validate end‑to‑end business processes
  • Leverage SAP’s solutions such as:
    • SAP Signavio for process visualization
    • SAP LeanIX for understanding IT landscapes and dependencies

Adopting a Clean Core Strategy for Long‑Term Stability

With the rise of cloud technologies, companies can innovate at a faster pace than ever before. Within this context, maintaining a clean core becomes essential. By minimizing custom code in the core system, organizations can ensure stability, simplify upgrades, and improve extensibility.

A clean core keeps your cloud environment streamlined and efficient, avoiding the unnecessary complexity that often results from excessive customizations.

👉How to maintain a clean core:

  • Use SAP for Me and SAP Cloud ALM to track system health and core integrity
  • Prefer configuration over customization whenever possible
  • Use SAP Business Technology Platform for modular extensions
  • Implement strong governance for evaluating and approving changes

A clean core plays a key role in reducing technical risks and building operational resilience, innovation readiness, and total cost efficiency.

Project risks may arise not only from technical gaps, such as lack of know‑how, product issues, or configuration challenges, but also from misalignment, late decisions, or communication gaps. These are best addressed by setting up proper Change Management alongside traditional Project Management.

Managing People‑Related Risks: Addressing the Human Side of Change with Organizational Change Management

Project management and change management play different but complementary roles. Cloud transformation affects people just as much as processes, and without a clear change management methodology, even small adjustments can meet resistance and slow progress.

Establishing a well‑defined change management strategy helps address these concerns and supports the human side of the project.  SAP supports this with the Organizational Change Management Road Map Viewer (S-User required), aligned with SAP Activate and filled with templates and accelerators. Effective change management improves communication, supports training, and reduces unexpected scope changes.

👉How to prevent resistance to new cloud processes:

  • Run early change and readiness assessments to understand your organization’s capacity for change
  • Create a compelling change story that explains the shift from current state to future benefits
  • Set up a communication strategy for continuous, transparent collaboration between stakeholders (e.g., business and IT), supporting feedback loops and efficient updates

 Security and Data Compliance Risks: A Part of Your Project That Cannot Be Overlooked

Security, privacy, and compliance are critical topics that must be addressed early. Delays in these areas can significantly impact project timelines and outcomes.

SAP provides the Trust Center, your central platform for security and compliance, delivering full transparency and easy access to SAP trust-related documents. In the Trust Center, you can access information on cloud service status, security, privacy, compliance, cloud operations, data centers, and agreements.

👉Some fundamentals when it comes to security:

  • Identify internal security, compliance, and data protection contacts early
  • Share the SAP Trust Center with them to provide access to key documents and certificates
  • Join SAP’s Security Community to stay informed about best practices
  • Subscribe to the SAP Security Products and Solutions Newsletter to keep up with product updates, events, and relevant news

 User Adoption and What Happens After Go‑Live

A technically sound system cannot deliver value if end users are unsure, unprepared, or unconvinced. When IT and business stakeholders don’t collaborate closely, organizations may encounter inconsistent usage, incomplete data entry, or a continued reliance on old tools.

By establishing a strong Change Management foundation, you prepare the way for successful user adoption after go‑live. That includes:

  • Nominating Product Ambassadors who champion adoption and gather ongoing feedback
  • Addressing user concerns proactively and incorporating feedback into project decisions
  • Defining metrics and KPIs to measure successful adoption (for example, the Consumption tab under Finance & Legal in SAP for Me provides insights on productive system usage)
  • Considering WalkMe as a digital adoption platform, offering interactive guidance to support onboarding and daily use

By taking these points into account, you set your project on the right path to success, helping you navigate and minimize risks throughout your cloud transformation.

 

Want to learn more?

📢Hear from our experts in the CX Onboarding: Managing Project Risks webcast — check our schedule.
📞You can also schedule a 1:1 call with one of our team members.

Key Resources

  • SAP Activate Road Map Viewer
  • Discovering SAP Activate - Implementation Tools and Methodology
  • Adoption Best Practices Video and Presentation
  • Organizational Change Management Road Map Viewer

We’d love to hear from you! Visit our Onboarding Resource Center, or feel free to contact us: SAP_CX_Onboarding@sap.com.

entry.title: Explain Central finance important things.
entry.content:
➢ Introductions: - With Central finance we can transition to SAP S/4 HANA without disruption to our current system landscape, which can be made up of a combination of sap system of different releases. This allows you to establish a central reporting platform for FICO with the option of creating a common reporting structure.

➢ Central Finance structure: -

 

Lest say we have SAP system of any released and non-SAP system, The Data is extracted to these servers to SLT server called “SAP landscape Transformation replication server”, It than goes to the different processes like first accounting interface of central finance where a lot of business mapping rules are defined, and we need to spend an ample of time during implementation. If records do not fall under the mapping rule, then it should be landed at error handling call AIR“Application Interface Framework” & if records are good to go as per business mapping rule, then document posted in Central finance ACDOCA – Universal Journal through internal accounting interface.

Why we plan for central finance?
Ans. Companies has multiple ERP systems, Companies doing multiple merger and Acquisitions, Companies have multiple non-SAP systems, non-disruptive structure and leading Finance First as single reporting structure.

➢ Key Important Things: - During implementation of central finance, financial transactions from the source are replicated to Central finance and most important and critical area is Master data.

▪ Financial & Non-Financial Master data
          • GL Account – Financial
          • Cost Element – Financial
          • Cost Centers – Financial
          • Profit Centers – Financial
          • Internal Order and Production Order etc. – Financial
          • Customer and Vendor (BP) – Non-Financial
          • Plants - non-financial
          • Materials – non-financial

▪ Initial Load: - Based Financial and Non-financial data we will plan initial load to CFIN system and some key important things we need to take care.

▪ Field Status Group: T code OBC4: - Filed status group determines which filed are suppressed, required or optional during document entry. If source has different field than target, then consider setting target as optional. Important you can map both system is same but more issue if mismatched Require and suppress Vs Other status between source and target system.

▪ Open item management: - Always consider Open item mgt. to Open Item Mgt. and Non-Open item Mgt. to non-open item Mgt… Check SAP notes based on system if ECC check 175960 – Activation of open item management and for S4 2745769 – Activation of open item
management. Program- FINS_SWITCH_TO_OPEN_ITEM: -

 

 

▪ Primary cost element: - Primary cost and Revenue includes P&L account like expenses, depreciation, salaries, revenue.

 

 

▪ Secondary cost element: - Secondary cost element are now GL.

 

 

▪ Non-Operating account: - P&L without cost element is now non-operating account and it may be use for Treasury, Foreign exchange gain & loss and Work in progress business scenarios.

 

 

▪ Reconciliation Account: - Reconciliation accounts are GL account to which postings are made automatically whenever a business transaction is entered on a subledger account (Such as accounts receivable, Account payable, and Fixed asset) Asset accounting is not supported in CFIN, the asset reconciliation accounts are mapped to non-reconciliation accounts. Replicated FI documents from asset postings are posted to general ledger in CFIN.
Entries available in ACDOCA table. Customer / Vendor reconciliation accounts are mapped to reconciliation accounts in CFIN.

 

▪ Tax category: - Source and target tax category should be same as Input, output and empty including posting without tax allow field check box ticked and not tucked.


▪ GR/IR Clearing account: - If business require enhancement, then check SAP note 165692 – Documentation Enhancement LMR1M002.

Note: - Once mappings are done and initial load also done then SAP provide important transaction code to check the loaded data status in CFIN.

 

▪ Margin Analysis: - Margine Analysis is huge topic but I will cover key important things. ACODA single source of truth.

 

 

 

 

▪ Initial load: - First reduce number of open items in source system before starting initial load and use transaction code like F-32, F-44 , F-03, F.13, FB1SL etc.

Initial load setting defines in table VCFIN_SOURCE_SET.

 

➢ AIF “Application Interface framework”: - AIF is powerful tool that helps execute error handling. Standard reports are below.

 

 

➢ Key takeaway & Important sap notes: - Data mapping and cleansing, change
management & ACDOCA is the universal journal.
Important SAP notes are…………. https://support.sap.com/
          • 2148893 - Central Finance: Implementation and Configuration
          • 2184567 - Central Finance: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
          • 2863836 - Allowed Currency Configuration Settings in Central Finance
          • 3194497 - Error Resolution Guides for S/4HANA Central Finance
          • 2610660 - SAP LT Replication Server for Central Finance - Third-Party System Integration 1709, FPS 2 and 1809           
         • 2932517 - Central Finance Third-Party System Interface: Frequently Asked
Questions (FAQ)
                     >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>END>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>

entry.title: DigiCert G5 Certificate Warning in /SDF/ALM_DIAGNOSTIC
entry.content:
Earlier this year, my team and I expaded our work with SAP Cloud ALM, integrating it with S4.  During the first configuration, we faced a persistent certificate warning related to DigiCert G5, and the health monitoring integration was impacted.

What made the situation intriguing was that STRUST was correctly maintained, the connection was technically active, yet the warning remained. Since there is already plenty of configuration content available, I decided to document this specific scenario to help others who may encounter the same behavior.

I hope this experience can be useful for people dealing with similar integration scenarios.

Context

During the integration between S/4 and SAP Cloud ALM, we noticed that Health Monitoring data was not updating as expected.

The configuration had been completed properly:

  • Transaction /SDF/ALM_SETUP executed
  • Certificate installed and validated in STRUST

However, when running /SDF/ALM_DIAGNOSTIC, a certificate warning related to DigiCert G5 was still displayed.

Symptom

  • Certificate warning message persists in /SDF/ALM_DIAGNOSTIC
  • Health Monitoring data was not updating consistently
  • Integration technically active but inconsistent

     

Root Cause Analysis

What was actually happening was related to the Health Monitoring framework delivered with certain ST-PI levels. There were inconsistencies in how monitoring data was collected and validated.

SAP Note 3639977 does not directly fix the certificate warning. Instead, it stabilizes several parts of the Health Monitoring logic.

Solution

The issue was resolved after implementing the SAP Note (3639977).

Steps performed:

  1. Implement SAP Note via SNOTE
  2. Transport adjustment 
  3. Re-run /SDF/ALM_DIAGNOSTIC
  4. Deactivate Health Monitoring data collection, wait approximately 20 minutes, and reactivate.
  5. Validate Health Monitoring data in SAP Cloud ALM

After implementation:

  • Monitoring data started updating correctly
  • Health Monitoring became consistent and reliable

Additional Correction (SAP Note 3426408)

After stabilizing the Health Monitoring behavior, the certificate warning /SDF/ALM_DIAGNOSTIC still showing.

To solve that, it was necessary to implement NOTE 3426408. This note enables the required diagnostic checks in the managed system and ensures that the certificate validation in STRUST is properly executed.

Conclusion

After the SAP notes implementatios, the system is now properly integrated and performing the required diagnostic validations as expected.

I hope this small step contributes to the community and that we can continue exchanging knowledge around S/4 and ALM

My question is why is it not allowing me to post the goods reversal? and how to overcome this issue?

Thanks a lot for your insights and support.

MM (Materials Management)

entry.title: Enterprise AI in Action: SAP Joule Delivers Where Others Don’t
entry.content:
AI is becoming part of everyday enterprise work, from drafting emails to designing complex processes. While ChatGPT and Gemini can generate functional specs and ABAP code, the real question is: “Why use SAP Joule?”
The difference is context – Joule is embedded in S/4HANA, understanding system configuration, business rules, workflows, and live data to deliver outputs that are accurate, compliant, and ready for real-world use. Unlike general-purpose AI, it doesn’t just produce well-written text; it grasps the technical and business landscape to ensure every recommendation aligns with your enterprise environment.
To understand this difference in practical terms, let’s look at two common S/4HANA scenarios: a Purchase Order compliance check and a Sales Order credit validation. On the surface, both appear straightforward – define the requirement, identify the enhancement spot, document the logic, and generate the necessary ABAP code.
A general-purpose AI tool can certainly help structure the functional specification and outline the technical approach. However, the real challenge in enterprise projects is not drafting the document – it is ensuring that the design aligns with the system’s actual configuration, enhancement framework, workflow setup, authorization model, and business data. This is where the distinction between generic AI and embedded, SAP-native intelligence becomes clear.

1. Purchase Order Compliance Check
Business Requirement: Block Purchase Order creation if:
• Vendor is not approved in a custom Z-table
• PO value exceeds 1,000,000
• Purchasing group = “A01”
• Mandatory attachment is missing
Using Chatgpt or Gemini:
• Enhancement via BAdI ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST
• Logic to check vendor approval, PO value, purchasing group, and attachment
• Error messages and test cases
Pros: Clear FS structure, general logic explained
Cons: Generic — it won’t know your actual Z-table structure, workflow configuration, or document type setup. You must feed these details manually.
Using SAP Joule:
• Identifies the correct BAdI or workflow in your S/4 system
• Pulls actual vendor master and custom table fields
• Checks your approval strategy and document type configuration
• Generates FS logic aligned with your live system setup
Pros: System-grounded, accurate, reduces rework, supports compliance

2. Sales Order Credit Validation
Business Requirement: Prevent Sales Order creation if a customer’s credit limit is exceeded.
Using Chatgpt or Gemini:
• Identify a relevant BAdI or user exit
• Check credit exposure against the limit
• Trigger an error message if the limit is exceeded
• Suggest test cases for validation
Pros: Clean structure, well-written, and easy to understand
Cons: Generic — it doesn’t know your actual credit control area, company code setup, or the exact fields and BAdIs in your S/4HANA system. All system-specific details must be manually added.
Using SAP Joule:
• Detect the active BAdI or enhancement spot in your system
• Pull the correct credit control configuration and field names
• Align logic with company code settings and authorization roles
• Suggest tests based on your live system data
Pros: Context-aware, system-aligned, lower risk of errors

Key Differentiators

  1. Verified SAP Knowledge Base: Responses are grounded in SAP's official documentation and verified sources.
  2. SAP-Specific Methodology: Understands SAP implementation approaches, configuration requirements, and technical constraints.
  3. Industry-Specific Compliance: Aligned with SAP's consulting standards and best practices.
  4. SAP Ecosystem Integration: Deep understanding of how SAP modules, technologies, and processes interconnect.

Comparison Table

Capability

SAP Joule for Consultants

ChatGPT

Gemini

Notes

SAP Domain Expertise

Deep, verified SAP knowledge from official sources

General SAP knowledge from public training data

General SAP knowledge with real-time search

Joule is trained on SAP-verified documentation

Template Generation

SAP-specific templates and methodologies

Generic business templates

Generic business templates with Google integration

Templates follow SAP best practices and standards

Functional Specification Writing

SAP implementation-focused with proper technical structure [3],[4]

General functional spec approaches

Structured but generic approaches

Joule understands SAP-specific requirements and constraints

Industry Standards Compliance

SAP best practices and implementation standards

General industry standards

General standards with research capabilities

Aligned with SAP's consulting methodologies

Technical Documentation Structure

SAP-aligned formats (ABAP, configuration paths, transaction codes) [2],[5]

Generic technical documentation

Structured technical docs

Joule provide SAP-specific technical details and paths

Integration Knowledge

Deep SAP ecosystem and module integration expertise

Broad but surface-level SAP integration knowledge

General integration knowledge

Joule understand SAP module interdependencies and best practices

Business Process Understanding

SAP module-specific processes and workflows

General business process knowledge

General business knowledge with research

Joule trained on SAP business process documentation

For functional specification writing in SAP contexts, Joule is the superior choice because:

  • Accuracy: knowledge comes from verified SAP sources rather than general internet training data. 
  • Relevance: understands SAP-specific technical requirements, configuration paths, and implementation constraints. 
  • Structure: can provide proper SAP documentation formats, including transaction codes, customization paths, and technical specifications. 
  • Best Practices: aligned with SAP's consulting methodologies and implementation standards.

We’ve seen how generic AI can draft specs and code, but context matters. In your S/4HANA projects, where has AI helped — and where has it fallen short? Share your experiences, challenges, or tips below!

Source:

SAP Help Portal | SAP Online Help

Joule

Other Blogs:

From Conversational AI to Business AI: Why SAP Jou... - SAP Community

System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA - SAP Community

entry.title: Re: Service Order Determines Wrong Plant for Service Part...
entry.content:
How SAP Determines the Plant for Service Parts

For service parts in a service order, the plant is not inherited directly from the service order header. Instead, SAP determines the plant based on:

Material Master Data
The plant assignment in the product master (material master) is the primary driver. If the material (e.g., Generator Oil) is maintained in plant 1010, the system will default to that plant, even if the service order header is in plant 1020.

Plant Determination Logic
SAP uses the logistics plant determination rules for service parts, which differ from service labor items. The system checks the material master and organizational assignments rather than the employee location or service team.

Employee Service Location / Service Team
These influence the service plant for labor items, but not for service parts. Service parts are always tied to material master plant data.

Why You’re Seeing the Error

Your service order header correctly determines plant 1020. But the service part item (Generator Oil) is maintained in plant 1010 in the material master. This mismatch creates an intercompany posting (1010 → 1020), which is not supported for service parts in Public Cloud. Hence, the release fails with the error message.


How to Ensure Service Parts Use the Same Plant

To align service parts with the service order plant (1020):

1) Extend the Material Master to Plant 1020
Maintain the service part (Generator Oil) in plant 1020. Ensure stock, valuation, and procurement views are set up for 1020.

2) Check Default Plant Determination Settings
In configuration, verify that the default plant determination for service parts points to the same plant as the service order. This is usually controlled by organizational data setup (company code ↔ plant assignments).

3) Cross-Company Assignments
Ensure that the material is not restricted to a different company code’s plant (1010). Service parts must belong to the same company code/plant as the service order.

Key Takeaway

  • Service parts derive their plant from the material master, not from the service order header.
  • To fix your scenario, you need to maintain the service part material in plant 1020 so that the system doesn’t default to 1010 and trigger an intercompany scenario

entry.title: Re: Set visible dependent on selected members in Input Co...
entry.content:
Hello @PaulaSAC ,

What you need is absolutely possible. 

1. Is this possible using an onSelect script on my input control, or do I need to read from a table?

You can use the onSelect method from your input-contol, you don't need to read from a table. 

2. How can I read the selected member from an input control or table?

You can read all about the different functions in the official script reference, but the way to read the values is :

var selectedCountry = InputControl_2.getInputControlDataSource().getActiveSelectedMembers();

Note: Even if your input-selection is set to "Single Selection", the function will always return an array. 

So, to get the value, you have to fetch the first element of that array:

//Get All Values:
var selectedValues = InputControl_2.getInputControlDataSource().getActiveSelectedMembers();
console.log(selectedValues); //Display Array - Only for demonstration :) 

//Get first element in array:
var selectedCountry = selectedValues[0];
console.log(selectedCountry); //Display Selected Value - Only for demonstration :)

Which will give you the value:

You can then use selectedCountry.id in an IF or switch statement.

//Get All Values:
var selectedValues = InputControl_2.getInputControlDataSource().getActiveSelectedMembers();
//Get first element in array:
var selectedCountry = selectedValues[0];
switch (selectedCountry.id) {
	case 'France':
		//do something
		break;
	case 'Germany':
		//do something else
		break;
	default:
		//do something	
}

3. How do I use setVisible to display the correct image?

Now, I don't know how your report is set up, but the "Image" object itself does not have a setVisible method, as you can see here.

However, you can wrap the images in a Panel, which does:

...
case 'France':
		Panel_12.setVisible(true);
		break;
	case 'Germany':
		//do something else
		break;
	default:
		//do something

That will hide the panel and all the content.

 

Hope this helps! 🙂
